utterances: It has been a tough eight years, but we're very proud of the president and the tough choices he's made, and feel like he's been a wonderful president.
The Bush house is a red-brick, one-story ranch with slate tile roof.
But the home's modest curb appeal hides its true size and luxurious appointments.
D Magazine reporter Candy Evans, whose online blog is called ""Dallas Dirt,"" broke the biggest story of her career when she scooped where the Bushes were going to live.
Everyone wanted to see.
It is 8,501 square feet, probably a his and her office, and you have a couple of guest rooms.
There are quarters above the garage, which can be developed into, you know, a guest house or security detail.
Eighty-five hundred square feet, I know it sounds like a lot, but actually that's not a really huge home.
When you start talking 15 and 20 thousand, those are the big mamas.
The Bush property backs up to that of one of Dallas' richest men, Tom Hicks.
Hicks owns the Major League Baseball team the Texas Rangers, the hockey team the Dallas Stars, and the European soccer team Liverpool FC, among other substantial holdings.
There's a rumor that now that the Bushes have moved next door, the billionaire is planning on building a helipad for their convenience.

But outside the Republican enclaves of Preston Hollow, the ranch in Crawford, and his hometown of Midland in West Texas, where Mr. Bush grew up, Texans' feelings about the president are considerably more diverse.
Bruce Buchanan is a government professor at the University of Texas, an expert on presidential politics.
He wanted to be a difference maker as president.
He wanted to be a transformational leader and not a steady-state or incremental leader.
And by golly he threw a lot of long passes, although most of them were incomplete.
In Dallas, Mr. Bush will have a new presidential library to build and a controversial legacy to defend.
But first, George and Laura Bush will begin their new life by spending some quiet time in the familiar surroundings of their hill country ranch.
Wade Goodwyn, NPR News, Dallas.
NPR's business news starts with the other bailout.
(SOUNDBITE OF MUSIC)
It came after a lot of lobbying by Detroit.
Yesterday, the House of Representatives approved a measure to fund $25 billion in low-interest loans for U.S.
automakers.
The measure is part of a larger spending bill that the Senate could vote on as early as this week.
The money is supposed to be used to retool old factories and produce more fuel-efficient cars.
Michigan lawmakers asked for the money, saying it'd save jobs and the American auto industry.
If the measure passes, it would be the first government intervention in the auto industry since the $1.2 billion bailout of Chrysler back in 1980.
NPR's business news starts with a possible lifeline for Washington Mutual.
(Soundbite of music)
The country's largest savings and loan says it will receive a $7 billion cash infusion to shore up its finances.
The money is coming from a group of investors led by the giant private equity firm TPG.
Washington Mutual has been struggling with huge losses from its mortgage business, and it's already cut thousands of jobs and will cut 3,000 more.
The lender also says it will reduce its mortgage operations.

He writes about medicine for the online magazine Slate, and he joins us now from his office in Woodbridge, Connecticut.
Hi, Syd.
Hi, Madeleine.
Still, it would not be the first time that Mother Nature has been called into the service of international espionage.
Joining us now is Peter Earnest, the executive director of the International Spy Museum here in Washington, D.C.
He spent 20 years in the CIA and was a member of the CIA's senior intelligence service.
He's with us here in studio 3A.
Thanks very much for joining us today.
Yes.
Thank you for inviting me, Neal.
Does using a rock sound improbable to you?
Well, I noticed in your introduction you said it sounded like it came out of Q's laboratory.
It sounded a little bit more like Monty Python to me.
I have to tell you, I have very few details about this rock.
Mm hmm.
I hope we have a picture of somebody trying to lift it up.
There was a picture on Russian television.
And they show a picture of what they say is a British secret agent coming up and trying to pick up the rock.
And apparently it's quite heavy.
OK.
It is heavy.
Yes.
It looks heavy.
Yes.
It looks heavy.
All right.
Well, there are all sorts of stories in the world of espionage about using things like rocks.
Well, what do they use them for?
Well, no, I mean, it depends how big the rock is.
In communicating with agents, for example, we often used rocks, things that looked like rocks, even animal carcasses.
So, they simply looked like things that were cast-offs in the street, an old rag, carcass of an animal, something like that.
And it might signal you to go to the post office to pick up a message or something?
Well, it wouldn't signal you to do anything.
It's not a signal.
What it would do is conceal whatever it is we're trying to pass.
Ah ha.
Now, whether that's, you know, gold or diamonds or messages or you name it, passports or whatever.
The key to Fort Knox, no doubt.
Or the key to Fort Knox.
But typically these things, sometimes called dead drops, well, you remember in the Ames case here in Washington...
Aldrich Ames, the famous traitor.
Yes, infamous, and also Hanssen, the FBI turncoat.
You remember both of them engaged heavily in leaving things.
Hanssen left things under bridges which all had to be retrieved, and Ames also had signal sites near the Soviet Embassy and elsewhere and would leave things to be picked up and retrieved.
Your most critical role in agent operations is communications.
How do you pass things back and forth?
If your agent can't pass you current intelligence then you're going to get dated intelligence, which is not worth very much.
We're talking about inanimate espionage.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Now, you mentioned carcasses and rocks.
Any other unusual objects have been used in this way?
Well, we've got, I can give you two examples from the International Spy Museum down the street where I just came from.
We have a tree stump there that looks like a tree stump.
But what it was, it was a device for communicating messages that it had stored up and looks, if you were to walk in the woods, you wouldn't notice it.
But there is under, in that, within that tree stump, the capability of communicating.
There's even one closer to what you're talking about in one sense.
There is what, it looks like a dog excrement, all right, doo-doo, in other words.
In common (unintelligible), yes.

And what it contained was a homing device, a beacon, so that if you left that in a select area, say on the Ho Chi Minh Trail or somewhere, aircraft or even ground troops could hone in on that in terms of directing fire.
There again, it was designed to look like something out of nature, which, if you were to walk by it, you would have no interest or desire to pick up.
Is there a danger that there might be pitfalls here?
I mean, somebody might say, you know, remove the rock or discover things that are going on?
EARNEST?
Oh, sure.
And that's happened all the time.

I mean, I can't tell you how many agent operations in the early years, certainly, of CIA where we would put concealment sites or something in a building and low and behold two or three years later you'd go there and the building was gone you deal with that all the time.
Urban renewal is a big problem.
Urban renewal is a big problem.
There's always been this Monty Python aspect.
There's always been the potential for this Monty Python aspect to intelligence because things happen.
So, even with the best-laid plans of mice and men, of putting down devices, of measuring and so forth and so on, buildings get removed, walls get taken down, accidents happen.
Is this, do you think, a case of, you know, old thinking, old espionage, living out of a digital age?
I mean, now with wireless digital technology, why are we still using rocks?
Big rocks?
I honestly don't know because I don't know what is alleged to have happened.
In other words, has somebody come outside a building, picked up a rock and taken it inside?
Yes.
Yes, that's apparently what happened.
This case does have a serious side.
OK.
